concept_name: The Resultant Formula — |R| = sqrt(A^2 + B^2 + 2AB cos theta)
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.2 — Addition of Vectors; split from vector_addition bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: Two farmers in a village in Punjab pulling a heavy plough with separate ropes, each at a different angle. The plough moves along a single direction — the resultant — whose strength depends on BOTH the individual pulls AND the angle between the ropes. Close the angle to zero and the plough flies forward; spread the ropes wide and the effective pull drops sharply.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A boy flies a kite on Makar Sankranti. The kite experiences horizontal wind pressure AND a vertical pull from his string. The kite's net force — what actually holds it aloft at a specific tilt — is the vector resultant of these two.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: You row a small boat across the Ganga. Your paddle pushes the boat forward at four metres per second; the river current pushes it sideways at three metres per second. Your boat does not travel at seven metres per second diagonally — it travels at five, because these velocities combine by the resultant formula, not by plain addition.

formulas.resultant_magnitude: |R| = sqrt(A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B cos theta)
formulas.law_of_cosines_link: Derived from the law of cosines on the triangle formed by A, B, and R

constraints: Formula applies to ANY two coplanar vectors A and B meeting at angle theta
At theta = 0, reduces to R = A + B (parallel)
At theta = 180, reduces to R = |A - B| (antiparallel)
The 2AB cos theta term is the 'correction' that pure scalar addition misses
Symmetric in A and B — confirms A + B = B + A (commutative)

STATE_1.title: Two Pulls, One Plough — Where Does It Go?

scene_composition.text:
The plough moves in ONE
direction, with ONE net pull.

How strong is that pull?

Naive guess: 4 + 3 = 7 N.
Reality: it depends on the
angle between the two ropes.

tts_sentences.text_en: Picture two farmers pulling a heavy plough in a village field, each with a separate rope tied to the same plough.
Farmer one pulls with four newtons, slightly upward from straight ahead. Farmer two pulls with three newtons, at a wider angle.
The plough cannot obey two masters. It moves in one direction, with one effective force. That single force is called the resultant.
A student's first guess is four plus three equals seven newtons. Let us see why that guess is wrong the moment the two ropes are not parallel.
The correct answer needs a single formula — and the next three states will build it from scratch.

STATE_2.title: Build the Parallelogram — The Diagonal Is the Answer

scene_composition.text:
Parallelogram law

Draw A and B from a common tail.
Complete the parallelogram
(two dashed copies).

Draw the DIAGONAL
from the shared tail to the
opposite corner.

That diagonal IS R.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the rule for combining two vectors into one, and it is purely geometric.
Draw A and B starting from the same point, each at its own angle.
Now make parallel copies — one copy of A starting at the tip of B, one copy of B starting at the tip of A. The dashed arrows show this.
You have just closed a parallelogram. Four sides. Two tails meeting at the start, two tips meeting at the opposite corner.
Draw the diagonal from the shared tail all the way to that opposite corner. This diagonal is the resultant R.
Its length gives the magnitude of R. Its tilt gives the direction. Our job in the next state is to find a formula for its length.

STATE_3.title: Drop a Perpendicular — The Law of Cosines Emerges

scene_composition.text:
Right triangle (Pythagoras):

R^2 = (A + B cos theta)^2 + (B sin theta)^2
    = A^2 + 2 A B cos theta
      + B^2 cos^2 theta + B^2 sin^2 theta
    = A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B cos theta
      (cos^2 + sin^2 = 1)

=> R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B cos theta)

tts_sentences.text_en: Now we need the length of the diagonal R. Geometry gives it to us in one step.
From the tip of B — the far corner of the parallelogram — drop a perpendicular onto the line of A extended.
The vertical drop has length B sine theta. The horizontal span from the origin out to the foot of the perpendicular has length A plus B cosine theta.
These two segments meet the diagonal R in a right triangle. Now apply Pythagoras.
R squared equals A plus B cosine theta squared plus B sine theta squared.
Expand the first square. Collect terms. Cosine squared plus sine squared is one, so the B squared pieces add up neatly.
What drops out is the formula you will use for the rest of your life — R squared equals A squared plus B squared plus twice A B cosine theta.

STATE_4.title: Plug In Numbers — A = 4, B = 3, theta = 60 deg

scene_composition.text:
Given  A = 4 N, B = 3 N, theta = 60 deg

Step 1  cos 60 = 0.5
Step 2  R^2 = 16 + 9 + 2 * 4 * 3 * 0.5
            = 16 + 9 + 12
            = 37
Step 3  R = sqrt(37) ~ 6.08 N

Naive scalar sum would give 7 N.
Gap of nearly a full newton
=> real world is not scalar addition.

tts_sentences.text_en: Let us use the formula on the two-farmer scene that started this lesson.
A is four newtons. B is three newtons. The angle between the ropes is sixty degrees.
Cosine of sixty is half, which is the cleanest number in all of trigonometry.
R squared is sixteen plus nine plus twelve, which is thirty-seven. Take the square root.
The real resultant is about six point zero eight newtons. The naive seven was wrong by nearly one whole newton.
This is the formula in action. Carry it forward — it will solve every vector-addition problem you meet for the rest of the chapter.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Two vector magnitudes can be added like ordinary numbers
trigger_phrases: A and B together is A plus B

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — Just Add the Numbers

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

A + B = 4 + 3 = 7 N

Simple. Familiar. Clean.
And — completely wrong
whenever the angle is not zero.

tts_sentences.text_en: The student's first attempt is always scalar addition. Four plus three is seven.
That answer is correct for mass, for time, for money. Scalars add by plain arithmetic.
Vectors do not, except in one special case — when the two vectors are exactly parallel. Any other angle, and the naive sum overshoots reality.

STATE_2.title: See the Gap — Reality at theta = 60 deg

scene_composition.text:
Correct formula
R^2 = 16 + 9 + 12 = 37
R = 6.08 N

Naive sum
R_naive = 7 N

The red arrow overshoots reality
by nearly a full newton.

The gap comes entirely from the
middle term 2 A B cos theta
that scalar addition forgets.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch both predictions side by side at sixty degrees.
The green arrow is what the formula gives — six point zero eight newtons, tilted upward from A.
The red dashed arrow is what naive addition claims — seven newtons pointing straight along A. Longer, and pointing the wrong way.
The two differ because scalar addition forgets the middle term, two A B cosine theta. That term carries all the geometry.
When the angle is non-zero, that missed term is non-zero, and the two predictions diverge.

STATE_3.title: When Scalar Addition Does Work — The Limit Case

scene_composition.text:
When theta = 0
cos theta = 1

R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B
    = (A + B)^2

=> R = A + B

Scalar addition IS correct,
but only in this special case.

tts_sentences.text_en: Your scalar instinct is not useless. It becomes exactly correct in one special case — when the two vectors point in the same direction, theta equals zero.
Plug zero into the formula. Cosine zero is one. The middle term becomes two A B. The whole expression collapses to A plus B whole squared.
Take the square root and R is simply A plus B. Four plus three is seven, and this time it is right.
So your original rule was not wrong — it was just incomplete. It only applies in one corner of the possible angle range.

STATE_4.title: The Correct Rule You Now Own

scene_composition.text:
Two vectors at angle theta

Never just add magnitudes
(except at theta = 0).

Use the formula:
R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B cos theta)

At theta = 90 deg  -> R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2)
At theta = 180 deg -> R = |A - B|
Everywhere in between -> use the full formula

Rule of thumb

If the angle is NOT explicitly zero,
DO NOT add magnitudes.

If unsure about the angle,
use the formula — it is always safe.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the complete picture in one frame.
For any angle, the resultant magnitude follows the full formula — A squared plus B squared plus twice A B cosine theta, all under a square root.
At zero degrees, it collapses to plain addition. At ninety degrees, to Pythagoras. At one hundred eighty, to the absolute difference.
Rule of thumb — if the problem mentions an angle that is not zero, stop writing plus and start writing the formula.

regeneration_variants.label: Different magnitudes and angles
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 5 N, B = 12 N, theta varying. Resultant ranges from 17 N (theta = 0) down to 7 N (theta = 180).
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Law of cosines R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B cos theta) applies unchanged

regeneration_variants.label: Velocities instead of forces
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 3 m/s (boat paddle), B = 4 m/s (river current), angle 90 deg. Resultant velocity is 5 m/s at 53 deg.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Formula is unit-agnostic — any two coplanar vectors combine by the same rule
